title: Chowla's conjecture on correlations of the Liouville function
statement: For the Liouville function λ(n) = (−1)^Ω(n), every non-trivial correlation vanishes: for distinct non-negative integers h₁,…,h_k and exponents a₁,…,a_k ∈ {1,2} not all even, (1/x)·Σ_{n≤x} λ(n+h₁)^{a₁}⋯λ(n+h_k)^{a_k} → 0 as x → ∞. Equivalently, λ is a normal ±1 sequence — it exhibits no correlation at any finite scale. Chowla implies Sarnak's Möbius disjointness conjecture (Möbius disjointness of every zero-entropy, i.e. deterministic, topological dynamical system); in logarithmically averaged form the two are equivalent (Tao–Teräväinen). Proved so far only in averaged form: the logarithmically averaged two-point case (Tao 2016) and all logarithmically averaged odd-order cases (Tao–Teräväinen 2018). Every unaveraged case with k ≥ 2, and the logarithmically averaged even orders k ≥ 4, are open.

unknowns.description: a proof that every non-trivial k-point correlation of λ vanishes (or shifts whose correlation does not)

criteria.test: an accepted proof that every non-trivial k-point correlation of λ tends to 0, unaveraged, for all k
exhibit shifts h₁,…,h_k and exponents whose correlation does not tend to 0

refs: S. Chowla, The Riemann Hypothesis and Hilbert's Tenth Problem (1965) — the conjecture as usually stated
P. Sarnak, Three lectures on the Möbius function, randomness and dynamics (2011) — Chowla ⇒ Möbius disjointness for zero-entropy systems
T. Tao, The logarithmically averaged Chowla and Elliott conjectures for two-point correlations, Forum Math. Pi 4 (2016), arXiv:1509.05422
T. Tao, R. Teräväinen, Odd order cases of the logarithmically averaged Chowla conjecture, J. Théor. Nombres Bordeaux 30(3) (2018)
T. Tao, Equivalence of the logarithmically averaged Chowla and Sarnak conjectures, arXiv:1605.04628

title: Beck–Fiala conjecture
statement: Is there an absolute constant C such that every finite set system in which each element lies in at most t of the sets has combinatorial discrepancy at most C·√t? Equivalently: for every hypergraph of maximum degree t there is a ±1 colouring of the ground set leaving every edge with imbalance O(√t), with no dependence on the number of elements or sets. The Beck–Fiala theorem (1981) gives the dimension-free bound 2t−1, improved to 2t − log*t by Bukh (2016); Banaszczyk's convex-geometry argument gives O(√(t log n)) at the cost of a dependence on the number n of elements, sharpened in 2025 to O(√(t log log n)) in the regime t ≥ log⁵n. The conjectured dimension-free O(√t) is open.

unknowns.description: a ±1 colouring achieving O(√t) discrepancy for every degree-t set system (or a family of set systems forcing more)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of an O(√t) discrepancy bound independent of the size of the ground set
exhibit degree-t set systems whose discrepancy is ω(√t)

refs: J. Beck, T. Fiala, "Integer-making" theorems, Discrete Appl. Math. 3 (1981) — discrepancy ≤ 2t−1
B. Bukh, An improvement of the Beck–Fiala theorem, Combin. Probab. Comput. (2016), arXiv:1306.6081 — 2t − log*t
W. Banaszczyk, Balancing vectors and Gaussian measures of n-dimensional convex bodies (1998) — O(√(t log n))
An Improved Bound for the Beck-Fiala Conjecture, arXiv:2508.01937 (2025) — O(√(t log log n)) for t ≥ log⁵n

title: Four exponentials conjecture
statement: If x₁, x₂ are complex numbers linearly independent over ℚ and y₁, y₂ are complex numbers linearly independent over ℚ, then at least one of the four numbers e^{x₁y₁}, e^{x₁y₂}, e^{x₂y₁}, e^{x₂y₂} is transcendental. The corresponding statement with a third ℚ-independent y — the six exponentials theorem (Siegel, Schneider, Lang, Ramachandra) — is a theorem; Lang observed that its proof "just misses" the 2×2 case, and closing that gap has been open since. The sharp and strong four-exponentials variants (Waldschmidt) are likewise open.

unknowns.description: a proof that one of the four exponentials is transcendental for every pair of ℚ-independent pairs (or a counterexample making all four algebraic)

criteria.test: an accepted proof that for all ℚ-independent (x₁,x₂) and (y₁,y₂) some e^{xᵢyⱼ} is transcendental
exhibit ℚ-independent (x₁,x₂) and (y₁,y₂) with all four e^{xᵢyⱼ} algebraic

refs: S. Lang, Introduction to Transcendental Numbers (1966) — the six exponentials theorem and the 2×2 gap
K. Ramachandra, Contributions to the theory of transcendental numbers (1968)
M. Waldschmidt, Diophantine Approximation on Linear Algebraic Groups (2000) — four exponentials, sharp and strong variants
